problem.value: Avoid leave requests stalling when the manager is unavailable, ensuring timely processing and compliance with leave-balance rules.
problem.evidence: Request explicitly asks for 'escalation if the manager is away' — implies a current blocking risk.

current_process.evidence: Not stated whether an approval circuit already exists or this is net-new.

success_metrics.evidence: No SLA or turnaround target given (e.g., 'no request pending > 48h').

actors.value: Employee (requester), Manager (primary approver), unnamed escalation approver.
actors.evidence: Explicit: manager approves. Escalation approver identity not specified.

business_objects.value: Leave/Absence request linked to Employee, balance/quota, Manager hierarchy.
business_objects.evidence: Domain context: leave requests carry balance/quota checks by default.

business_rules.evidence: Unclear how 'manager is away' is detected (own approved leave record? manual flag? out-of-office calendar?) — this is the core rule driving the whole feature.

workflow.value: Pending → Approved/Rejected, with an Escalated state triggered when manager is unavailable.
workflow.evidence: Escalation implies an added state and transition; trigger type (sync/async) and timing undefined.

integrations.evidence: Notification channel/recipient for escalation not specified.

permissions.evidence: Unclear if escalation approver gets full approve/reject rights or limited visibility; approval circuits are shared/sensitive per context.

config_vs_custom.evidence: Escalation rules and delegates are typically client/team-specific per platform's configurability model.

constraints.value: Likely subject to French labor law leave-approval timing expectations.
constraints.evidence: Context notes French labor law governs paid leave/RTT.

edge_cases.evidence: No mention of overlapping absences, no delegate configured, multiple simultaneous escalations.

reporting.evidence: No explicit audit requirement stated yet, but HR/leave data is regulated (GDPR, labor law) — may need an approval-history trail.

risks.evidence: Not defined; likely risk of double-approval or missed escalation if trigger logic is unclear.

questions.q: How is 'the manager is away' detected — their own approved leave record in the system, a manual out-of-office toggle, or an external calendar/HR flag — and is escalation immediate or after a delay (e.g., no action within 48h)?
questions.why: High impact × high uncertainty: this rule defines the entire trigger mechanism (sync vs async), timing, and data source — the core of the feature, currently undefined.

questions.q: Who is the escalation approver — a pre-designated delegate chosen by the manager, the manager's own manager (skip-level), or HR — and does this vary by team, country, or contract?
questions.why: High impact × high uncertainty: determines routing logic and whether config-per-client is needed; escalation target is unnamed in the request.

questions.q: Does the escalation approver get the same rights as the original manager (approve/reject, see balance/quota) or only visibility/override rights?
questions.why: High impact: approval circuits and permissions are shared/sensitive across modules — an under-specified escalation role risks unintended access.

questions.q: Should the escalation rule (who escalates to whom, after how long) be configurable per team/country, or is one fixed rule enough for all clients?
questions.why: High impact: platform is multi-client/configurable by design; approval circuits are already noted as client-specific — hardcoding here could require rework later.

questions.q: Does the leave module need an audit trail showing who approved/escalated each request and when (for HR compliance / labor-law traceability)?
questions.why: Medium-to-high impact if compliance applies: HR data is regulated (GDPR, French labor law) — an audit requirement changes reporting from a nice-to-have to a build requirement.

summary.objective: Ensure employee leave requests are never blocked by an absent manager, by adding an escalation path to the existing (or new) approval workflow.
summary.scope: Extend the leave approval workflow with an 'escalated' state, a rule to detect manager unavailability, a designated escalation approver, and appropriate notifications/permissions — likely reusing the platform's existing approval-circuit and balance/quota logic.
assumptions: An approval circuit for leave already exists and only needs an escalation branch (not a full new workflow).
Manager unavailability will be detected via their own leave/absence record in the system.
Escalation approver is a single role (e.g., skip-level manager) rather than a configurable list.
Standard balance/quota checks already apply and are unaffected by escalation.
summary.blind_spot: The trigger for 'manager is away' is undefined — whether it's automatic (based on the manager's own approved absence) or manual, and whether escalation fires immediately or after a delay. This single rule determines if the build is a simple state addition or a more complex async/timed workflow with its own edge cases (overlapping absences, no delegate configured, double escalation).
